sentences.text: Those trips were-- many trips during the day, as many trips as they-- we were on 12 hour shifts.
So as many trips as we could make during the day, we went back and forth, going through that gate, where you go out of the camp, bringing in the barrack into the camp.
Every time we came through that gate back into the camp, there were always two, three, eight-- there were people just dropping.
I-- I surmise now that this was their first taste of the camp working.
People were not used to this kind of-- of hard labor.
And on top of everything else, they was-- they wouldn't let us drink any water.
One prisoner couldn't-- went to a-- as we marched by saw a barrel of water.
He ran and he put his face into the water to drink.
The SS just went past after him and just pushed his head into the water until he couldn't breathe anymore.
And just took his hand off and the guy fell down.
He was dead.
He-- he-- I forget the word.
When you can't breathe anymore--
He suffocated.
As we were building these barracks, whenever a barrack got finished and completed, other prisoners walked-- were moved into it.
The-- the Jewish prisoners were living in the barracks while it was being built.
When a barrack was completed, other prisoners moved into it.
And this pattern repeated itself.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a while, they assigned-- we're still the same group, the ones that are still there are still part of the same group that we started out with from our town-- from the camp-- from the ghetto.
We're still together, most of the same group.
The rabbi of our-- of Stramtura, Hersh Kahan, is with us.
He's one of the prisoners, no different than anybody else.
He's still-- he is still with us at that point.
Some of the--
We were-- we were together-- we hadn't been separated at this point yet.
We were still together with him.
No, no, no, no, there was no-- no way, there was-- we-- we didn't even many times knew when-- when Shabbos was or when a holiday was.
We just-- there was no way of doing any sort of observing.
What-- the rabbi was still with us at this point.
Some of the people that-- from the town, some adults from the town would go over to the rabbi and they would ask him, can we eat some of that soup that they're giving us?
They-- they were concerned and they-- it's not kosher.
And he tell-- he told the people, everybody is on his own.
It's a matter of survival, if it's-- it's a survival of the fittest.
And you just do what your heart tells you to do.
Later on, we-- we get assigned to different work details.
Some of us are assigned to dig, what I would call, integration ditches.
They're giving-- we're going to work with pick and shovel.
Regardless of the age, regardless of the condition of the prisoner, we're given a quota.
Each team, one guy with a pick and one guy with a shovel, has to dig a so-- something, a ditch that is so long by so wide and so deep.
I don't remember.
And the-- that-- that was in centimeters and meters, whereas we're in inches and feet today.
And I-- I can't translate it.
If you didn't finish the quota today, you got the same quota tomorrow, and you had to finish from the day before.
Some of us that couldn't, like I couldn't-- I was a kid-- we would trade bread for-- for-- with stronger people.
We would trade our-- our ration or part of our bread for people that were stronger and they were able to do-- work a little harder and faster than we were able to.
We-- we worked on that-- some of us worked on that.
I work with this-- on this-- we called it kommando.
The kommando is a group of people that work in a certain place.
Some of us were in other kommandos.
Some of them were assigned to-- to work in the tunnel.
And they had an armband that had a V1 on it or a V2 on it.
When they worked on the V1 and the V2, we did not know what that meant.
But there were prisoners there, non-Jewish prisoners that were prisoners of war or political prisoners that they knew what-- what V1 and V2 meant.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At this point--
They-- there were 12 hour shifts.
And they-- they were day shift and night shift.
The-- that place never stopped.
It was 12 hour shifts.
And people, when they-- they came out, they came out into the camp and slept in the-- in the camp.
And they would walk out through that gate that said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
They walked out. 12 hours later they would walk back in again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After we were-- after we were in Dora for maybe a month or so, they started to send transports.
Dora was a satellite camp, and it had other little camps around it.
Two names come to mind.
I don't-- I remember one was called Harzungen.
And one was called Ellrich.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the group that we were still together, including the rabbi from our-- from our town went to one or the other, either Harzungen or Ellrich.
A few of us were still left in Dora.
I was one of them.
There was-- they may have had a-- a method of selecting.
I don't know.
We just-- we were standing on-- on Zahlappell, and they would call prisoner number so-and-so report to a certain spot, and so on and so forth.
And they had a group they would send out.
We didn't know where they were going.
But they wound up, we found out later on, on little satellite camps that belonged to Dora.
Some of these people were working in the tunnel on the opposite side that we entered from the Dora side.

And they were working with these jackhammers, digging, where they were telling me that at the end of the day, they would be all white from the dust of the stone from by digging with that vibration thing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm still-- I'm still in Dora.
And I'm still working in that kommando that we were digging these irrigation ditches.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In this case, it was just a matter of luck how you were assigned to different work details.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took me off from that work detail.
And they put me in to work in a-- in a nursery.
I want to tell you this camp had maybe 20,000 inmates.
We had-- there was a jail in the camp.
And there were-- most-- a lot of people worked in the tunnel.
People worked on the railroad-- on the railroad siding.
I was-- after that commander that we were digging ditches, I was put him to work in a nursery.
There was no-- they don't ask-- they didn't ask for any-- they didn't ask where you want to go.
They tell you where you want to go.
It was-- they had a-- in the camp, they had sort of a, what you would-- equivalent to what you would call here an unemployment office or an employment office.
And they assigned people as they needed in different places.
I was assigned to this nursery.
And this nursery used human waste for fertilizer.
Our job was-- they-- they teamed us up in teams of two.
There were boxes which looked like a-- like a wheelbarrow.
Only a-- a wheelbarrow has two handles and a wheel.
This box had two handles on each side without a wheel on it.
Labor-- they had bodies for labor enough, so they didn't-- they didn't use wheelbarrows.
They used humans for labor.
So we were carrying that stuff all day long, back and forth.
It got to the point where we couldn't carry it.
It was hard already, so we found a wire.
And we would put a piece of wire on from one handle of the-- of the-- that box to the other, where we would hold out-- where we would pick it up with our hands.
And we would rest the wire on our back, so it would take some-- some of the weight of our hands.
That was considered, mind you, a better job than the job before.
We were treated a little better.
Most of-- most of this job was given to kids my age or a little older.
From the human-- from the-- from the prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had out houses, where-- the prisoners had out houses.
There was no facilities in our-- in our barracks.
And they would use that.
The kapo that was our kapo at that time was a-- a German who was-- who-- he was in for-- for-- he was a criminal.
We could see on his-- on his collar.
He-- he was already in the camps at that point, at least, he told us, about six or seven years at that point already.
He was-- he was a very strict man.
But he was, compared to other kapos, treating other prisoners, he-- he didn't treat us too bad.
We worked like this for some time.
When we used to get fed in the morning, we used to get a-- a third of the loaf of bread.
As I say, the bread was always the size of a brick.
The team used to get-- the team that worked on the same-- carrying the same box was-- got to be close, like partners.
Most of us that got the bread in the morning, if you ate it up right away, you didn't have anything else for the rest of the day.
So we would split-- the team, we would split one of-- one of our pieces of bread in the morning.
And we would keep one of our pieces of bread for later on when we got hungry during the day.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were people that were heavy smokers.
And they were looking for cigarettes.
And some other [? were ?] tobacco or people rolled their own, I don't know how they got it, but some people gave their bread away so they could have-- so they can buy a cigarette or two.
And that meant life or death.
Yes, I do.
He lives in-- I haven't seen him in many years.
He lives somewheres in New York.
Huna Malik.
No relationship.
We just met there.
And we were teamed up.
And we got to be partners in this carrying of this box all day long.
Most of us-- none of us had any-- any kind of personal problems with each other, except there were people that would steal bread from other people.
That was-- people would want to push in line while we were standing to receive our soup, people would push in front of each other.
But other than that, there was-- there was not a problem between the prisoners, we-- among the prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After I was working there for a while, and it was-- it wasn't a pleasant job, I was assigned to the SS kitchen.
I was doing the dirty work, like washing pots and pans, peeling potatoes.
And that was a lifesaver to me, because they had all the food that they-- food that we forgot exists, like sugar, eggs, bread, the best-- the best that they could get.
They had a kitchen.
And-- and the prisoners had a kitchen.
There were two different kitchens.
And their kitchens, suffice-- suffice to say, is-- was a lot different than the prisoners' kitchen.
I-- I learned the ropes to steal food and bring it in to my fellow friends and people that I knew.
Bread, sugar, butter, whatever I can put my hands on.
And other people like me that worked in that kitchen did that.
And I had my sort of my group of kids that-- and people that I helped with food.
I didn't know everybody in that-- in that detail.
But I would say 50, 60 of us.
Most of us got that job because they were young, my age.
They called jungendlich.
That's youngsters.
It was a softer job.
And it was given mostly to younger people like my age at that time.
So I used to help my friends, people that I know.
There was one particular man about my father's age.
His name was [?
Barak ?] [?
Stegmann ?] that I met in the ghetto.
And I sort of took to him because he was my father's age.
And my father wasn't with me, and I sort of clung, as much as possible, with this guy.
What would you talk about together?
I didn't know him before the war.
We met in the ghetto.
But we would talk about how sweet life used to be.
And this man, the way he told me, he was a well-off man back home.
And we would reminisce about good old days.
He had a-- he had a saying when we were out and-- and the sun was shining.
He used to say, what a false sun it is.
It was shining, but we were all in-- in the camps.
And he would say, what a false sun that looks down on us.
Something to that effect, I don't remember verbatim.
[SIGHS] Well, we were-- we were pretty much knocked out.
I know I-- I used to sleep-- I used to sleep through the night.
There were elderly or older people than I was at that time that I would hear sighing and I would hear sort of subdued crying.
But we-- we slept through the night.
The-- as the war kept advancing, we didn't have any kind of news from the outside world.
The Allies used to come through with hundreds of planes going on bombing missions.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They had two ways of-- the siren had two ways of letting us know when the planes are approaching was like three long blasts.
And when the planes were like overhead, they had like a-- a crying, wailing blasting of the-- of the sirens.
So we knew the planes were far-- further yet or we knew the planes are already almost on top of us.
They must have known the camp, because they never bombed the camp.
But they bombed the railroad siding and the land outside of the camp.
But the barracks are so thin that when they were bombing, the barracks would vibrate.
The first time this ever happened, we never had any experience with bombing and-- and that kind of situation, we all-- when-- we all sort of hid under the beds as-- as if this would save us.
We could not go-- get out of the barracks.
So we were all-- it was dark out.
It had to be a blackout.
That was the orders from the SS.
But because it was-- it was a-- a very dreary time, especially the first time this happened, we heard the explosions of the bombs.
And we were all like crouching under the-- under the beds.
And it was as quiet as a-- you could hear a pin drop.
Everybody was just having their own thoughts.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] One of the guys started to sing 'My Yiddishe Momme,' just-- he broke the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] [SIGHS] he broke the silence.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [SIGHING] That song, I've heard that song sung many times after.
But that song, that time really hit everybody.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We heard the siren again, sort of it's a-- we're-- we're out of range of the bombing and so forth.
It was close to a morning where we had to get up and Zahlappell.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] We all went back to work, to whatever work we were assigned to.
I continued doing what I did, bringing in food and helping my fellow prisoners, my friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] One day, they stopped and frisked us when we came in.
When we came in-- in-- the kitchen was outside of the camp, so we would have to go out through the gate.
And coming back from work we would come back through the gate.
And we would stand again, Zahlappell.
They would count us again.
And then they would let us go to sleep for the night.
One day, I came through the gate, and they frisked us.
And they found bread, potatoes, whatever I was able to-- I used to put it into my shirt.
And it used to be-- I used to look like a fat man, loaded with whatever, raw potatoes and bread, whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I-- I and others that were caught like me did that, we were dismissed from that job.
We couldn't-- they couldn't do-- we couldn't work there anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And we were put into a-- a V1, V2 kommando.
Life is still going on in the camp, the-- the same routine as before.
Only one day, they're making us, they're making all-- the whole camp stand at attention, just like at-- at an appell.
And they bring out two prisoners.
Their hands were tied in the back.
And we see the gallows, the rope with a noose standing there with a bench for the prisoners to stand up on.
And all of us had to stand there in a circle, and we all have to watch.
One of the prisoners started to talk.
And he was telling us not to worry, to hold out, the war is lost, the Germans are going to lose.
And as he was starting to talk, the SS stopped him, stifled him.
The man didn't have anything to lose anymore anyway.
He knew in a few minutes he's dead.
Where he was talking as much as he could until he was stopped.
And the two prisoners were hung.
That's the first time that I saw an execution of this kind in the camp.
I saw that people-- I got used to it.
I was hardened already by that point to see dead bodies.
But I never saw anybody get hung before.
Several times after that, within a period of several weeks in-- in between, some of the prisoners that worked in those V1 and V2 place knew what they were doing.
And they sabotaged the work.
Because they sabotaged the work, they were put in-- they were put in-- there was a jail in the camp.
They were put into jail.
The night before they would gag them.
They would put a-- a gag in their mouth because of what happened before when that guy was talking, I guess.
So with that gag all night in their mouth-- it must have been done real tight-- when they came out to be hung, they were all swollen around this gag.
They couldn't talk.
They tried to.
I remember hearing like muffled noises that they wanted to say something.
But they couldn't because the gag was in their mouth.
Things got worse, where the first time we saw two.
The-- the next time we saw seven guys coming up.
And there was only four gallows.
And the others see the first ones go on and they're waiting to be next.
I can imagine what-- what-- what they thought waiting in a few minutes they'll be dead.
We all had to watch it, the whole camp, everybody.
It was a must.
This happened several times within a period of a couple of months.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are at this point, we're working in the tunnel.
It's a 12-hour shift.
We go out every morning, come back 12 hours later.
It-- it depends-- within the-- within these two kommandos, it depends what work you were given at.
Some of us were-- were working in-- in sort of an assembly line that was pretty good.
The tunnel had ventilation and everything else.
Some of us were working with those jackhammers, where there was no masks.
And they were-- we were shaking as the-- as the jackhammer went.
And all that dust settled on us.
And when we came home at the end of the day we were all white from the-- from the stone dust.
